relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: YHWH speaks a ritual for returning from contact with death.
A red cow without defect or yoke is chosen for the rite.
Eleazar brings her outside the camp.
Blood is sprinkled seven times toward the tent.
The whole animal is burned before the priest.
Cedar, hyssop, and scarlet enter the fire.
The priest washes and carries temporary impurity until evening.
The one who burns the cow also washes and waits.
A clean person gathers ashes for future water of purification.
Even the ash-gatherer becomes temporarily unclean; Israelite and sojourner share the statute.
Touching a human corpse creates seven days of ritual impurity.
The third and seventh days mark the path back to ritual cleanness.
Refusing the rite leaves the sanctuary boundary unresolved.
Death within a tent affects everyone inside and entering.
Open vessels share the tent's ritual exposure.
Battlefield death, bones, corpses, and graves all carry the same boundary.
Ashes and living water are joined in a vessel.
Hyssop carries the water across people, objects, and spaces touched by death.
Sprinkling, washing, bathing, and evening restore ritual access.
The unpurified person remains outside sanctuary participation.
Those who administer purification also bear temporary impurity.
Death-contact spreads through touch until evening.

contemplative_note: Numbers 19 treats death as a boundary that affects bodies, homes, tools, and communities. It does not call grief sinful. Its strange paradox is that those who help restore others also carry part of the impurity for a time.
